articles.headline: It's Not Just How Long You Sleep. It's How Consistent You Are.
articles.body: A massive new study of 88,461 adults found that irregular sleep patterns are linked to significantly higher risk across 172 diseases, including liver and circulatory disorders. The kicker: it wasn't just about duration. Going to bed and waking up at inconsistent times was a key driver of disease risk, possibly through inflammatory pathways. Researchers say it's time to redefine 'good sleep' to include regularity, not just the number of hours. Same bedtime every night is a bigger deal than most people think.
articles.takeaway: Pick a consistent sleep window and protect it like a meeting you can't cancel. Regularity may matter as much as duration.

product.name: LectroFan White Noise Machine
product.blurb: Blocks ambient noise so your sleep is actually restorative.

articles.topic: stress brain resilience pain tolerance FIU study
articles.headline: Your Brain Gets Tougher Every Time You Push Through Discomfort
articles.body: New research from Florida International University found that how your brain handles stress is less about how much pain you feel and more about how flexibly it responds. Using EEG, researchers found that people who used strategies like paced breathing, attention-shifting, and self-talk stayed engaged longer under stress. People who tried to force rigid control quit earlier. The brain, it turns out, builds new tolerance reference points each time you push through something hard — whether that's a cold plunge, a hard workout, or a tough week.
articles.takeaway: Cognitive flexibility under pressure is trainable. Next time a workout gets hard, try a quick reframe or breath reset instead of white-knuckling through.
articles.source: FIU News / Journal of Applied Physiology

articles.topic: gut bacteria depression inflammation Harvard Morganella morganii
articles.headline: A Gut Bacterium May Be Quietly Fueling Depression Through Hidden Inflammation
articles.body: Harvard Medical School researchers have identified a biological mechanism linking a specific gut bacterium, Morganella morganii, to depression. When this microbe interacts with a common environmental pollutant, it produces a molecule that triggers inflammation — a process strongly linked to depressive disorders. The findings, published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, offer a clearer window into how gut microbes can shape brain health at a molecular level and may point toward new treatment targets beyond the brain itself.
articles.takeaway: You can't control every pollutant, but a diverse, fiber-rich diet supports a gut environment less hospitable to harmful bacteria like this one.
articles.pexelsQuery: vegetables colorful salad bowl healthy food

articles.topic: FDA removes HRT black box warning menopause 2026
articles.headline: The FDA Just Quietly Changed the Game for Women's Hormone Health
articles.body: In February 2026, the FDA approved labeling changes that remove decades-old black box warnings from hormone replacement therapy products for menopause. The warnings on cardiovascular disease, breast cancer, and dementia risk have been stripped after a comprehensive review of the science. The FDA now notes that women who start HRT within 10 years of menopause onset may reduce heart attack risk by up to 50% and Alzheimer's risk by 35%. The old warnings, based on a flawed 2002 study, had caused HRT use to plummet from 27% to just 5% by 2020.
articles.takeaway: If you or someone you know is in perimenopause or early menopause, this changes the conversation with your doctor. Timing matters — the data supports starting earlier.

workout.title: Dead Hang and Row Back Builder
workout.warmup: Spend 90 seconds doing arm circles and shoulder rotations, then hang from the bar with a relaxed grip for 20 seconds. Follow with 10 slow cat-cow stretches to open up the thoracic spine before loading.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.notes: Hang with a full overhand grip, shoulders packed down away from ears. Builds grip strength and decompresses the spine. Beginner mod: bent-knee hang to reduce load.

exercises.name: Scapular Pull-up
exercises.notes: Start in a dead hang, then retract and depress your shoulder blades without bending your elbows. This activates the lower traps before full pull-ups. Keep it slow and controlled.

exercises.name: Bodyweight Row (under a table or low bar)
exercises.notes: Lie below a sturdy table edge or low bar, grip and pull chest to bar keeping body rigid like a plank. Easier: higher angle. Harder: feet elevated. Squeeze shoulder blades at the top.

exercises.name: Chin-up or Band-Assisted Pull-up
exercises.notes: Supinated (palms facing you) grip targets biceps and lats. Loop a resistance band over the bar and kneel in it for assistance. Focus on pulling elbows down and back, not just lifting chin.

exercises.name: Dead Bug (core anti-extension)
exercises.notes: Lie on back, arms extended to ceiling, knees at 90 degrees. Slowly lower opposite arm and leg toward the floor while pressing your lower back into the ground. Pause, return, switch sides.

workout.cooldown: Hang passively from the bar for 30 seconds to decompress, then spend 2 minutes in a doorway chest stretch with arms at 90 degrees to reverse the pulling pattern and reset posture.

product.name: Gymnastic Rings - Double Circle Wood
product.blurb: Chris trains on rings 2x per week. The most effective upper body tool that fits in a bag.

meal.title: Honey Garlic Salmon Rice Bowl
ingredients: 1 cup cooked brown rice or jasmine rice
meal.instructions: 1. Mix soy sauce, honey, garlic, and sesame oil. Marinate salmon for 5 minutes. 2. Cook salmon in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, basting with leftover marinade until caramelized. 3. Toss red cabbage with rice vinegar and a pinch of salt. Warm edamame in the microwave. 4. Build bowls: rice base, flaked salmon, edamame, and pickled cabbage. Top with sesame seeds and green onion.
meal.tip: Frozen salmon fillets work perfectly here. Thaw in cold water for 15 minutes while you prep everything else — no advance planning needed.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
